---
title: "Neural Networks"
subtitle: "BSAD 8310: Business Forecasting --- Lecture 10"
author: "BSAD 8310: Business Forecasting"
institute: "University of Nebraska at Omaha"
date: "Spring 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, emory-clean.scss]
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    incremental: false
    toc: false
    center: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.05
    logo: ""
    footer: "BSAD 8310 | University of Nebraska at Omaha | Spring 2026"
    embed-resources: true
bibliography: ../Bibliography_base.bib
csl: https://www.zotero.org/styles/chicago-author-date
---

## Lecture 10: Outline

1. Feedforward Networks: The Foundation
2. Recurrent Neural Networks
3. LSTM: Long Short-Term Memory
4. Attention Mechanisms
5. Keras Implementation
6. Application to Forecasting

---

## Motivation: Beyond Trees --- Sequential Patterns

::: {.keybox}
**Lecture 09 best result:** XGBoost, RMSE $\approx$ 2,250 on RSXFS.

XGBoost treats each row as independent. Lag features give it a *window* into the past, but the model itself has no memory --- it sees a feature vector, not a sequence.
:::

**What neural sequence models add:**

- **Explicit memory:** the hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ propagates information from *all* previous steps, not just the lag columns
- **Long-range dependencies:** structural breaks, multi-year trends, and recession shocks can be carried forward without requiring one lag feature per month
- **Learnable temporal weighting:** attention lets the model decide which past time steps matter most for each prediction

**Today's goal:** build from a single neuron to LSTM to attention, then compare against the full Lectures 01--09 leaderboard.

---

# Feedforward Networks

Building blocks of deep learning: neurons, activations, and multi-layer transformation

---

## From Linear Regression to a Single Neuron

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
**Linear regression** (from Lecture 02):

$$\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$$

**One neuron** adds a nonlinear activation $\sigma(\cdot)$:

$$a = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b)$$

::: {.smaller}

| **Activation** | **Formula** | **Use** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| ReLU | $\max(0,z)$ | Hidden layers |
| Sigmoid | $1/(1+e^{-z})$ | Gate outputs (LSTM) |
| Tanh | $\tanh(z)$ | Gate candidates |
| Linear | $z$ | Output (regression) |

:::
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Activation Function
:::
Without $\sigma(\cdot)$, stacking layers is equivalent to a single linear layer. Non-linearity enables neural networks to approximate arbitrary continuous functions (the *universal approximation property*).
:::

::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
Why ReLU?
:::
ReLU avoids the saturation-driven vanishing gradient problem of sigmoid/tanh in deep feedforward networks. Default choice for hidden layers.
:::
:::

::::

---

## A Feedforward Network for Forecasting

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 320 210" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:100%; max-height:200px; display:block; margin:0 auto; font-family:inherit;">
  <!-- Input layer (x=30) -->
  <circle cx="30"  cy="60"  r="16" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="30"  y="64" font-size="9" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">x₁</text>
  <circle cx="30"  cy="105" r="16" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="30"  y="109" font-size="9" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">x₂</text>
  <text x="30"  y="140" font-size="13" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">⋮</text>
  <circle cx="30"  cy="165" r="16" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="30"  y="169" font-size="9" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">xₚ</text>

  <!-- Hidden layer 1 (x=110) -->
  <circle cx="110" cy="42"  r="16" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <circle cx="110" cy="84"  r="16" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <circle cx="110" cy="126" r="16" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <circle cx="110" cy="168" r="16" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1.2"/>

  <!-- Hidden layer 2 (x=200) -->
  <circle cx="200" cy="63"  r="16" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <circle cx="200" cy="105" r="16" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <circle cx="200" cy="147" r="16" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1.2"/>

  <!-- Output layer (x=285) -->
  <circle cx="285" cy="105" r="16" fill="#FDECEA" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="285" y="109" font-size="9" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">ŷ</text>

  <!-- Connections: input -> hidden1 -->
  <line x1="46"  y1="60"  x2="94"  y2="42"  stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="46"  y1="60"  x2="94"  y2="84"  stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="46"  y1="60"  x2="94"  y2="126" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="46"  y1="60"  x2="94"  y2="168" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="46"  y1="105" x2="94"  y2="42"  stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="46"  y1="105" x2="94"  y2="84"  stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="46"  y1="105" x2="94"  y2="126" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="46"  y1="105" x2="94"  y2="168" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="46"  y1="165" x2="94"  y2="42"  stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="46"  y1="165" x2="94"  y2="84"  stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="46"  y1="165" x2="94"  y2="126" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="46"  y1="165" x2="94"  y2="168" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>

  <!-- Connections: hidden1 -> hidden2 -->
  <line x1="126" y1="42"  x2="184" y2="63"  stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="126" y1="42"  x2="184" y2="105" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="126" y1="42"  x2="184" y2="147" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="126" y1="84"  x2="184" y2="63"  stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="126" y1="84"  x2="184" y2="105" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="126" y1="84"  x2="184" y2="147" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="126" y1="126" x2="184" y2="63"  stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="126" y1="126" x2="184" y2="105" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="126" y1="126" x2="184" y2="147" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="126" y1="168" x2="184" y2="63"  stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="126" y1="168" x2="184" y2="105" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="126" y1="168" x2="184" y2="147" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>

  <!-- Connections: hidden2 -> output -->
  <line x1="216" y1="63"  x2="269" y2="105" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="216" y1="105" x2="269" y2="105" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="216" y1="147" x2="269" y2="105" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>

  <!-- Layer labels -->
  <text x="30"  y="198" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">Input</text>
  <text x="30"  y="208" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">layer</text>
  <text x="110" y="198" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">Hidden</text>
  <text x="110" y="208" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">layer 1</text>
  <text x="200" y="198" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">Hidden</text>
  <text x="200" y="208" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">layer 2</text>
  <text x="285" y="198" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">Output</text>
  <text x="285" y="208" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">layer</text>
</svg>
```

:::

::: {.column width="46%"}
**For RSXFS forecasting:**

- **Input:** lag features $\mathbf{x}_t = (y_{t-1},\ldots,y_{t-12},\text{dummies})$ --- a *flat* vector
- **Hidden:** $\mathbf{h}_k = \mathrm{ReLU}(\mathbf{W}_k \mathbf{h}_{k-1} + \mathbf{b}_k)$ at each layer
- **Output:** linear (no activation) for regression

::: {.warningbox}
A feedforward network treats its inputs as an *unordered* feature vector. It has no notion of time order --- $y_{t-1}$ and $y_{t-12}$ are just two columns. This is why we need recurrent architectures.
:::
:::

::::

---

## Training: What the Business Analyst Needs to Know

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Training Loop
:::
1. **Forward pass:** compute predictions $\hat{y}$
2. **Loss:** $\mathcal{L} = \mathrm{MSE}(y, \hat{y})$
3. **Backward pass:** compute $\nabla_\mathbf{W}\mathcal{L}$ via backpropagation
4. **Update:** $\mathbf{W} \leftarrow \mathbf{W} - \eta\,\nabla_\mathbf{W}\mathcal{L}$
5. Repeat for many epochs (passes over the training data)
:::

**Key hyperparameters:**

::: {.smaller}
- Learning rate $\eta$ (Adam default: 0.001)
- Batch size (16--32 for small $n$)
- Number of epochs (use early stopping)
:::
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
*The key practical skills are **not** deriving backpropagation --- they are choosing architecture depth, setting the learning rate, applying dropout, and recognizing overfitting early.*

::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
Adam Optimizer
:::
Adam adapts the learning rate per parameter using running estimates of gradient mean and variance. Default `lr=0.001` works well for most forecasting tasks.
:::
:::

::::

---

# Recurrent Neural Networks

Sequential memory through recurrent hidden states updated at each time step

---

## The Vanilla RNN: Sequential Processing

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
RNN Update Rule
:::
$$\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh\!\bigl(\mathbf{W}_h \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{W}_x \mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{b}\bigr)$$

$$\hat{y}_{t+1} = \mathbf{w}_y^\top \mathbf{h}_t + b_y$$
:::

**Key properties:**

- $\mathbf{h}_t$ is a compressed summary of all past inputs
- $\mathbf{W}_h, \mathbf{W}_x$ are *shared* across all time steps --- same transformation at every step
- $\mathbf{h}_0 = \mathbf{0}$ (no prior information at start)
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
Analyst Analogy
:::
An RNN reading retail sales month by month is like an analyst updating a running summary: each new month's number refines the mental model of where the trend is headed.
:::

::: {.warningbox}
Shared weights mean the same transformation applies at every step --- efficient but limiting for very long sequences where patterns at very different time scales must be captured.
:::
:::

::::

---

## RNN Unrolled Through Time

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 580 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:100%; max-height:185px; display:block; margin:0 auto; font-family:inherit;">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arr10rnn" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#aaa"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arr10rnn2" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#005CA9"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>

  <!-- Input nodes (y=160) -->
  <rect x="75"  y="144" width="68" height="30" rx="3" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="109" y="163" font-size="10" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">x_{t-2}</text>
  <rect x="245" y="144" width="68" height="30" rx="3" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="279" y="163" font-size="10" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">x_{t-1}</text>
  <rect x="415" y="144" width="68" height="30" rx="3" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="449" y="163" font-size="10" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">x_t</text>

  <!-- Hidden nodes (y=85) -->
  <rect x="75"  y="70"  width="68" height="32" rx="3" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="109" y="90" font-size="10" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">h_{t-2}</text>
  <rect x="245" y="70"  width="68" height="32" rx="3" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="279" y="90" font-size="10" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">h_{t-1}</text>
  <rect x="415" y="70"  width="68" height="32" rx="3" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="449" y="90" font-size="10" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">h_t</text>

  <!-- Output (y=20) -->
  <rect x="415" y="8"  width="68" height="30" rx="3" fill="#FDECEA" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="449" y="27" font-size="10" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">ŷ_{t+1}</text>

  <!-- h_0 initialisation -->
  <text x="22" y="90" font-size="9" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">h₀=0</text>

  <!-- Arrows: x -> h -->
  <line x1="109" y1="144" x2="109" y2="102" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#arr10rnn)"/>
  <line x1="279" y1="144" x2="279" y2="102" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#arr10rnn)"/>
  <line x1="449" y1="144" x2="449" y2="102" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#arr10rnn)"/>

  <!-- Arrows: recurrent h_{t-1} -> h_t -->
  <line x1="38"  y1="86" x2="75"  y2="86" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#arr10rnn)"/>
  <line x1="143" y1="86" x2="245" y2="86" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#arr10rnn)"/>
  <text x="194" y="80" font-size="9" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">W_h</text>
  <line x1="313" y1="86" x2="415" y2="86" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#arr10rnn)"/>
  <text x="364" y="80" font-size="9" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">W_h</text>

  <!-- Arrow: h_t -> output -->
  <line x1="449" y1="70" x2="449" y2="38" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#arr10rnn)"/>

  <!-- "Shared weights" note -->
  <text x="279" y="190" font-size="9" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">Same W_h, W_x at every time step</text>

  <!-- Time arrow -->
  <line x1="30" y1="198" x2="555" y2="198" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#arr10rnn2)"/>
  <text x="562" y="202" font-size="10" fill="#005CA9">time t</text>
</svg>
```

[*Socratic: the same $\mathbf{W}_h$ and $\mathbf{W}_x$ apply at every time step. What does this shared-parameter constraint imply about how the model treats January vs. July?*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

## Why Vanilla RNNs Fail on Long Sequences

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="54%"}
::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Vanishing Gradient
:::
[@Bengio1994] Backpropagating through $T$ steps requires multiplying $T$ copies of $\mathbf{W}_h^\top$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{h}_0} \propto \bigl(\mathbf{W}_h^\top\bigr)^T$$

If $|\lambda_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_h)| < 1$, this product $\to \mathbf{0}$ exponentially. Weights early in the sequence receive near-zero gradients --- the model *forgets* the distant past.
:::

[$\lambda_{\max}$ = spectral radius of $\mathbf{W}_h$; unrelated to regularization $\lambda$ from Lecture 08.]{.neutral .smaller}

**Consequences for forecasting:**

- A vanilla RNN trained on RSXFS cannot reliably learn that December sales are high because *December was high 12 months ago*
- The 12-month seasonal dependency is precisely where vanilla RNNs fail
:::

::: {.column width="42%"}
::: {.warningbox}
Vanilla RNNs work for 5--10 step dependencies. For monthly seasonality (lag 12) or multi-year cycles, they systematically underperform.
:::

*The fix: LSTM introduces gating mechanisms that protect long-range gradients by creating a direct **information highway** across time steps.*
:::

::::

---

# LSTM: Long Short-Term Memory

Gated cell state solves the vanishing gradient problem for long-range dependencies

---

## LSTM Gates: Forget, Input, Output

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="56%"}
::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
LSTM Gate Equations
:::
[@Hochreiter1997] Here $[\mathbf{h}_{t-1},\,\mathbf{x}_t]$ denotes horizontal concatenation. $\sigma$ = sigmoid (here specifically); $\odot$ = element-wise product.

$$f_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_f [\mathbf{h}_{t-1},\,\mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f) \quad\text{(forget)}$$

$$i_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_i [\mathbf{h}_{t-1},\,\mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i) \quad\text{(input)}$$

$$\tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_g [\mathbf{h}_{t-1},\,\mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_g) \quad\text{(candidate)}$$

$$o_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_o [\mathbf{h}_{t-1},\,\mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o) \quad\text{(output)}$$

$$\mathbf{c}_t = f_t \odot \mathbf{c}_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t$$

$$\mathbf{h}_t = o_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{c}_t)$$

Gates $f_t, i_t, o_t \in (0,1)^d$ are *vectors*; $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$ and $\mathbf{x}_t$ were defined in the RNN section. *(Cell data flow on next slide.)*
:::
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
**Forget gate $f_t$:**
*$f_t \approx 1$: keep memory; $f_t \approx 0$: erase. During a recession, the model learns to reset the normal-growth signal.*

**Input gate $i_t$:**
*Controls which new information enters cell state. A large December dummy triggers high $i_t$ to write the holiday pattern.*

**Output gate $o_t$:**
*Controls which part of cell state flows to $\mathbf{h}_t$ --- what is announced to the next layer.*
:::

::::

---

## LSTM Cell Architecture

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 500 230" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:92%; max-height:210px; display:block; margin:0 auto; font-family:inherit;">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arr10lstm" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#aaa"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>

  <!-- Cell state highway (top) labels -->
  <text x="12"  y="46" font-size="9" fill="#525252">c_{t-1}</text>
  <text x="464" y="46" font-size="9" fill="#525252">c_t</text>

  <!-- Forget gate box -->
  <rect x="62"  y="100" width="72" height="36" rx="4" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="98"  y="116" font-size="9" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">Forget</text>
  <text x="98"  y="128" font-size="9" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">gate f_t</text>

  <!-- Multiply op: c_{t-1} * f_t -->
  <circle cx="98" cy="42" r="14" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="98" y="47" font-size="14" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">⊙</text>

  <!-- Input gate box -->
  <rect x="178" y="80"  width="72" height="36" rx="4" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="214" y="96"  font-size="9" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">Input</text>
  <text x="214" y="108" font-size="9" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">gate i_t</text>

  <!-- Candidate box -->
  <rect x="178" y="136" width="72" height="36" rx="4" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="214" y="152" font-size="9" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">Candidate</text>
  <text x="214" y="164" font-size="9" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">c̃_t</text>

  <!-- Multiply op: i_t * c_tilde -->
  <circle cx="214" cy="42" r="14" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="214" y="47" font-size="14" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">⊙</text>

  <!-- Add op: new cell state -->
  <circle cx="292" cy="42" r="14" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="292" y="47" font-size="16" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">+</text>

  <!-- Output gate box -->
  <rect x="360" y="110" width="72" height="36" rx="4" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="396" y="126" font-size="9" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">Output</text>
  <text x="396" y="138" font-size="9" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">gate o_t</text>

  <!-- tanh of new cell -->
  <circle cx="396" cy="42" r="14" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="396" y="47" font-size="8" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">tanh</text>

  <!-- Multiply op: o_t * tanh(c_t) -->
  <circle cx="396" cy="158" r="14" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="396" y="163" font-size="14" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle">⊙</text>

  <!-- Inputs (left) -->
  <text x="12" y="120" font-size="9" fill="#525252">h_{t-1}</text>
  <text x="12" y="180" font-size="9" fill="#525252">x_t</text>

  <!-- Output (right) -->
  <text x="464" y="163" font-size="9" fill="#525252">h_t</text>

  <!-- Cell state highway connections -->
  <line x1="38"  y1="42" x2="84"  y2="42" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#arr10lstm)"/>
  <line x1="112" y1="42" x2="200" y2="42" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#arr10lstm)"/>
  <line x1="228" y1="42" x2="278" y2="42" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#arr10lstm)"/>
  <line x1="306" y1="42" x2="382" y2="42" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#arr10lstm)"/>
  <line x1="410" y1="42" x2="460" y2="42" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#arr10lstm)"/>

  <!-- c_t down to tanh -> mul3 -> h_t -->
  <line x1="396" y1="56" x2="396" y2="144" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#arr10lstm)"/>
  <line x1="396" y1="172" x2="460" y2="172" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#arr10lstm)"/>

  <!-- Forget gate connections from h, x -->
  <line x1="42"  y1="118" x2="62"  y2="118" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#arr10lstm)"/>
  <line x1="42"  y1="176" x2="50"  y2="176" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <line x1="50"  y1="176" x2="50"  y2="118" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <line x1="50"  y1="118" x2="62"  y2="118" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <line x1="98"  y1="100" x2="98"  y2="56"  stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#arr10lstm)"/>

  <!-- Input gate connections from h, x -->
  <line x1="42"  y1="118" x2="55"  y2="118" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="55"  y1="118" x2="55"  y2="98"  stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="55"  y1="98"  x2="178" y2="98"  stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#arr10lstm)"/>
  <line x1="42"  y1="176" x2="178" y2="154" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#arr10lstm)"/>

  <!-- Candidate connections from h, x -->
  <line x1="55"  y1="98"  x2="55"  y2="154" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="55"  y1="154" x2="178" y2="154" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#arr10lstm)"/>

  <!-- i_t * c_tilde circle (mul2) up to add op -->
  <line x1="214" y1="116" x2="214" y2="56"  stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#arr10lstm)"/>

  <!-- Output gate connections -->
  <line x1="55"  y1="130" x2="360" y2="128" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <line x1="396" y1="128" x2="396" y2="110" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#arr10lstm)"/>
</svg>
```

::: {.keybox}
The cell state $\mathbf{c}_t$ flows through with only $\odot$ and $+$ operations --- gradients can propagate back without shrinking to zero.
:::

---

## LSTM for Time Series: Practical Setup

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
LSTM Forecasting Setup
:::
1. **Reshape:** stack $T$ consecutive feature vectors into a 3-D array: $(\mathbf{x}_{t-T+1}, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_t)$
2. **Process:** LSTM updates $\mathbf{h}_s, \mathbf{c}_s$ at each step $s = t-T+1, \ldots, t$
3. **Predict:** pass final $\mathbf{h}_t$ to a Dense layer: $\hat{y}_{t+1} = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{h}_t + b$

Lookback window $T$ is the key design choice.
:::

**Architecture options:**

::: {.smaller}
- **Single LSTM layer:** short-run patterns
- **Stacked (2-layer) LSTM:** first = momentum, second = seasonal structure; often best for monthly data
:::
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
::: {.keybox}
For RSXFS with strong 12-month seasonality, use $T \geq 24$ (two full years) to give the LSTM enough context to learn the annual pattern.
:::

*Note: the first $T$ observations are consumed to build the initial sequence and cannot serve as targets. With $T = 24$, document this 24-month shift when reporting sample sizes.*
:::

::::

---

## LSTM Key Hyperparameters

::: {.smaller}

| **Parameter** | **Keras name** | **Typical** | **Effect** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Lookback window | `T` (manual reshape) | 24--36 | Longer = more seasonal context; larger model |
| LSTM units | `units` | 32--128 | More = higher capacity; overfit risk |
| LSTM layers | (stack 2) | 1--2 | Second layer captures longer patterns |
| Dropout | `dropout,` `recurrent_dropout` | 0.1--0.3 | Regularization; crucial for small $n$ |
| Learning rate | `lr` (Adam) | 0.001 | Default works; reduce if training unstable |
| Epochs | `epochs` | 50--200 | Use `EarlyStopping(patience=20)` |

:::

::: {.keybox}
With $n \approx 300$ monthly observations, LSTM is *data-limited*. Use small units (32--64), dropout ($\geq$ 0.2), and `EarlyStopping`. Larger capacity will overfit. Run 5 random seeds; report median RMSE.
:::

[*Socratic: LSTM dropout ($\geq$ 0.2) regularizes similarly to LASSO from Lecture 08. What is the key conceptual difference between dropout-based regularization and L1 penalty-based regularization (LASSO)?*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

# Attention Mechanisms

Direct access to any past time step without sequential compression

---

## Attention: What the Model Learns to Focus On

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 620 175" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:96%; max-height:160px; display:block; margin:0 auto; font-family:inherit;">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arr10att" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#aaa"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>

  <!-- x-axis -->
  <line x1="28" y1="120" x2="608" y2="120" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#arr10att)"/>
  <text x="614" y="124" font-size="9" fill="#525252">time step</text>

  <!-- y-axis -->
  <line x1="28" y1="125" x2="28" y2="10" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="4" y="70" font-size="9" fill="#525252" transform="rotate(-90,12,70)">weight α_s</text>

  <!-- Bar: t-12 seasonal spike (UNO blue) -->
  <rect x="32"  y="20"  width="30" height="100" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1"/>
  <!-- Bar: t-11 through t-2 (gray, small) -->
  <rect x="76"  y="109" width="28" height="11"  fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <rect x="118" y="111" width="28" height="9"   fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <rect x="160" y="108" width="28" height="12"  fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <rect x="202" y="112" width="28" height="8"   fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <rect x="244" y="110" width="28" height="10"  fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <rect x="286" y="111" width="28" height="9"   fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <rect x="328" y="109" width="28" height="11"  fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <rect x="370" y="112" width="28" height="8"   fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <rect x="412" y="110" width="28" height="10"  fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <rect x="454" y="107" width="28" height="13"  fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <!-- Bar: t-1 momentum (UNO green) -->
  <rect x="496" y="78"  width="30" height="42"  fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1"/>

  <!-- x-axis labels -->
  <text x="47"  y="136" font-size="8" fill="#005CA9" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">t-12</text>
  <text x="90"  y="136" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">t-11</text>
  <text x="132" y="136" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">t-10</text>
  <text x="174" y="136" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">t-9</text>
  <text x="216" y="136" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">t-8</text>
  <text x="258" y="136" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">t-7</text>
  <text x="300" y="136" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">t-6</text>
  <text x="342" y="136" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">t-5</text>
  <text x="384" y="136" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">t-4</text>
  <text x="426" y="136" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">t-3</text>
  <text x="468" y="136" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">t-2</text>
  <text x="511" y="136" font-size="8" fill="#15803d" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">t-1</text>

  <!-- annotations -->
  <text x="47"  y="14"  font-size="9" fill="#005CA9" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="700">seasonal</text>
  <text x="511" y="72"  font-size="9" fill="#15803d" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="700">momentum</text>
</svg>
```

::: {.smaller}
- **High weight at $t-12$:** attending to "same month last year" --- an LSTM must propagate this 12 steps; attention reads it *directly*
- **Medium weight at $t-1$:** short-run momentum
- **Near-zero elsewhere:** the model ignores irrelevant lags --- no manual feature engineering required
:::

---

## Attention: Formal Definition

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Scaled Dot-Product Attention (simplified)
:::
Let $\mathbf{H} = [\mathbf{h}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{h}_T]$ be the stacked hidden states and $\mathbf{q}$ the query at the current step:

$$\boldsymbol{\alpha} = \mathrm{softmax}\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{H}\mathbf{q}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right), \quad \mathrm{context} = \mathbf{H}^\top \boldsymbol{\alpha}$$

$d_k$ = key-vector dimension; $\sqrt{d_k}$ prevents the dot product from growing large and saturating the softmax. Weights $\boldsymbol{\alpha} = (\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_T)^\top$ sum to 1 (one scalar $\alpha_s$ per time step) and are learned end-to-end.
:::

*Simplified single-query form; @Vaswani2017 use a full multi-head $(\mathbf{Q},\mathbf{K},\mathbf{V})$ formulation.*

[*Note: $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ here denotes attention weights --- distinct from ETS smoothing $\alpha$ (L03), ECM adjustment $\alpha$ (L05), and Elastic Net mixing $\alpha$ (L08).*]{.neutral .smaller}
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
What Attention Solves
:::
In LSTM, information at step $t-12$ must survive 12 hidden-state updates and can get diluted. Attention *directly* reads $\mathbf{h}_{t-12}$ with weight $\alpha_{t-12}$ --- no intermediate compression.
:::
:::

::::

---

## Transformers and Practical Guidance

**The Transformer [@Vaswani2017]:**

- Originally designed for NLP (language translation)
- Replaces recurrence entirely with *multi-head self-attention*
- Parallelizes perfectly over time steps --- no sequential bottleneck
- Requires substantially more data than LSTM to train well

::: {.keybox}
**Practical guidance for business forecasting:**

- Monthly data, $n \approx 300$: Transformer is over-parameterized; LSTM $\pm$ attention is the practical sweet spot
- Daily/weekly data, $n > 5{,}000$: Transformer or Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT) is state-of-the-art
- Multi-series panel ($N$ stores $\times$ $T$ months): train one LSTM/Transformer on all series simultaneously
:::

[*Socratic: self-attention treats its inputs as an unordered set of vectors --- it has no built-in notion of sequence position. Why is **positional encoding** essential for Transformers?*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

# Keras Implementation

Two-layer LSTM for business forecasting with sequence reshaping

---

## LSTM in Keras: Minimal Working Example

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="49%"}
**Step 1: Build sequences**

```python
import numpy as np
from tensorflow import keras
from tensorflow.keras import layers

T = 24  # lookback window (months)

def make_sequences(X_arr, y_arr, T):
    """Reshape to (n_samples, T, n_features)."""
    Xs, ys = [], []
    for i in range(T, len(X_arr)):
        Xs.append(X_arr[i-T:i])
        ys.append(y_arr[i])
    return np.array(Xs), np.array(ys)

X_arr = X.values.astype('float32')
y_arr = y.values.astype('float32')
Xs, ys = make_sequences(X_arr, y_arr, T)

# Chronological split (no shuffling)
n = len(ys)
n_test = int(0.15 * n)
n_val  = int(0.15 * n)
Xs_tr = Xs[:n-n_test-n_val]
ys_tr = ys[:n-n_test-n_val]
Xs_va = Xs[n-n_test-n_val:n-n_test]
ys_va = ys[n-n_test-n_val:n-n_test]
Xs_te = Xs[n-n_test:]
ys_te = ys[n-n_test:]
```
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
**Step 2: Build and train**

```python
n_features = Xs_tr.shape[2]

model = keras.Sequential([
    layers.LSTM(64,
        return_sequences=True,
        dropout=0.2,
        recurrent_dropout=0.1,
        input_shape=(T, n_features)),
    layers.LSTM(32, dropout=0.2),
    layers.Dense(1)
])
model.compile(
    optimizer=keras.optimizers.Adam(learning_rate=0.001),
    loss='mse')

cb = keras.callbacks.EarlyStopping(
    patience=20,
    restore_best_weights=True)

history = model.fit(
    Xs_tr, ys_tr,
    validation_data=(Xs_va, ys_va),
    epochs=200,
    batch_size=16,
    callbacks=[cb],
    verbose=0)

y_pred = model.predict(Xs_te).flatten()
```
:::

::::

---

# Application to Forecasting

Comparing LSTM against the full Lectures 01--09 leaderboard on RSXFS

---

## When Does LSTM Help? Data Requirements

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
::: {.warningbox}
**LSTM vs. XGBoost on small datasets:** A two-layer LSTM with 64 units has ${\approx}\,50{,}000$ parameters. XGBoost with 500 trees and depth 4 is sparse by comparison. With $n \approx 300$, this is a fundamental parameter-to-data mismatch.
:::

**Rule of thumb:**

- $n < 500$ monthly: XGBoost wins (more reproducible)
- $n > 1{,}000$ daily/weekly: LSTM competitive
- $N$ series $\times$ $T$ months (panel): LSTM scales well
- Long-range patterns beyond 12 months: LSTM advantage grows
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
When LSTM Wins
:::
- Daily energy demand or stock prices ($n > 3{,}000$)
- Multi-store retail panel: one LSTM across 1,000 stores
- Sequences with multi-scale patterns not captured by a fixed lag window
:::

[*On RSXFS alone ($n \approx 300$), expect LSTM RMSE $\approx$ 2,100--2,600 across seeds. The median is competitive with XGBoost but with higher variance. Always report median over $\geq 5$ seeds.*]{.neutral .smaller}
:::

::::

---

## Full Model Leaderboard: Lectures 01--10

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
**Test-set results on RSXFS (24-month horizon):**

::: {.smaller}

| **Model** | **RMSE** | **MAE** |
|:---|---:|---:|
| Seasonal Naïve | 4,210 | 3,120 |
| SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1)$_{12}$ | 2,840 | 2,100 |
| Elastic Net ($\lambda^*$) | 2,540 | 1,890 |
| Random Forest | 2,380 | 1,760 |
| XGBoost (early stop) | 2,250 | 1,650 |
| LSTM (2-layer, $T\!=\!24$) | 2,180 | 1,600 |

:::

[*Values are illustrative. LSTM = median over 5 seeds. Strategy: direct multi-step (one model per horizon).*]{.neutral .smaller}
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
**Interpretation:**

- LSTM edges XGBoost by $\sim$70 RMSE --- a modest, seed-sensitive improvement
- XGBoost is more *reproducible* (deterministic at `random_state=42`); LSTM requires seed averaging
- Both require feature engineering; LSTM additionally needs sequence reshaping

::: {.keybox}
XGBoost remains the recommended default for monthly macro data. Use LSTM when you have daily data, panel data across many series, or need to capture complex multi-scale temporal patterns.
:::
:::

::::

---

# Takeaways and References

What we learned and where to go next

---

## Lecture 10 Key Takeaways

::: {.keybox}
1. **Feedforward networks** are universal approximators but treat inputs as unordered feature vectors --- no built-in time awareness.
2. **Vanilla RNNs** add sequential memory but suffer vanishing gradients [@Bengio1994] --- reliable only for dependencies $\leq 5$--10 steps back.
3. **LSTM** [@Hochreiter1997] solves this with three gates (forget, input, output) and a protected cell state that carries information over long sequences.
4. **Attention** [@Vaswani2017] lets the model directly weight any past time step. Transformers generalize this but require far more data.
5. **Data size is the key constraint:** on $n \approx 300$ monthly obs., LSTM's margin over XGBoost is narrow and seed-sensitive. Always report median over $\geq 5$ seeds.
6. **Practical rule:** use XGBoost as your default ML forecaster for monthly macro data; use LSTM when you have daily/weekly data, panel data, or multi-step horizons with long-range dependencies.
:::

**Preview of Lecture 11:** Feature Engineering --- lags, rolling statistics, calendar effects, and pipeline design for both tree-based and neural network forecasters.

---

## References {.smaller}

::: {#refs}
:::
